name: Expert Scores and Kappa Are Used to Evaluate Simulated Essay Score Preservation
statement: Wolfe and Barber evaluate LLM-simulated essays with expert scores, score-distribution checks, exact and adjacent agreement, weighted kappa, and realism ratings, showing that scoring agreement is a central quality signal for generated essays.
population_or_context: Simulated student essays for automated-scoring data augmentation.
quantitative_note: The study reports 96 real essays, 288 simulated essays, exact agreement, adjacent agreement, weighted kappa, score-distribution differences, and realism ratings.
caveats: The real essay source conditions the generated essay, limiting comparator independence.
Human score agreement does not by itself validate a downstream automated scorer.
evaluation_method: Expert scoring and realism judgments compare simulated essays with original source essay scores and generated-output plausibility.
reported_metrics: Exact agreement, adjacent agreement, weighted kappa, score-distribution differences, and realism ratings.
limitations: The public preprint reviewed here does not name the exact LLM version.

source_locator.section: Methods and results sections on scoring agreement and realism
source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: The finding paraphrases the simulated essay scoring and agreement metrics reported for generated essays.
